1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128

62	/-! ## §1. Codim-1 cycle group / Weil divisor group
63	
64	Hartshorne's condition `(*)` (II §6, p. 130) is: `X` is a Noetherian integral
65	separated scheme that is regular in codimension one. Under `(*)`, prime divisors
66	are closed integral subschemes of codimension one, and the local ring at the
67	generic point of a prime divisor is a DVR with quotient field the function field
68	`K(X)`.
69	
70	A prime divisor is encoded by its **generic point** together with the
71	codimension-one witness `Order.coheight point = 1`: the data field `point : X`
72	selects the generic point of the closed integral subscheme, and the predicate
73	field `coheight` enforces codimension one in the specialisation preorder
74	(Hartshorne II §6 p. 130; blueprint pin `def:prime_divisor`). Integrality of the
75	closure is automatic, so no separate integrality witness is needed.
76	-/
77	
78	/-- A **prime divisor** on a scheme `X`: a closed integral subscheme of codimension
79	one, encoded (for a scheme satisfying Hartshorne's condition `(*)`) by its generic
80	point. On a curve, prime divisors correspond bijectively to closed points.
81	
82	The codimension-one witness is the predicate `Order.coheight point = 1` on the
83	specialisation preorder of `X.carrier` (Mathlib's convention is
84	`x ≤ y ↔ y ⤳ x`, so the generic point is the unique maximal element and a
85	generic point of a codim-1 closed integral subscheme has a length-one chain
86	above it). The closure `{point}̄` is automatically irreducible (so the reduced
87	induced subscheme structure is automatically integral); hence no separate
88	integrality field is needed.
89	
90	Blueprint reference: `def:prime_divisor` / `def:codim1_cycles` (Hartshorne II §6
91	p. 130; Stacks 02RW). -/
92	structure Scheme.PrimeDivisor (X : Scheme.{u}) where
93	  /-- The generic point of the closed integral subscheme. -/
94	  point : X
95	  /-- Codimension-1 witness: `point` has coheight `1` in the specialisation
96	  preorder on `X.carrier`. Per iter-173 `wd-spec-refine` (`def:prime_divisor`). -/
97	  coheight : Order.coheight point = 1
98	
99	/-- The **Weil divisor group** of a scheme `X` satisfying Hartshorne's condition
100	`(*)`: the free abelian group on the set of prime divisors of `X`,
101	`Div(X) = ⨁_{Y prime divisor of X} ℤ · Y`. An element `D = Σ nᵢ · Yᵢ` with finitely
102	many nonzero coefficients is a **Weil divisor**; if all `nᵢ ≥ 0` it is **effective**.
103	
104	Blueprint reference: `def:codim1_cycles` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 130). -/
105	def Scheme.WeilDivisor (X : Scheme.{u}) : Type u := X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ
106	
107	namespace Scheme.WeilDivisor
108	
109	noncomputable instance (X : Scheme.{u}) : AddCommGroup X.WeilDivisor :=
110	  inferInstanceAs (AddCommGroup (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ))
111	
112	instance (X : Scheme.{u}) : Inhabited X.WeilDivisor :=
113	  inferInstanceAs (Inhabited (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ))
114	
115	end Scheme.WeilDivisor

117	/-! ## §2. Order of a rational function at a prime divisor
118	
119	For a scheme `X` satisfying `(*)`, every prime divisor `Y` carries a discrete
120	valuation `v_Y` on the function field `K(X)`. The order of a nonzero rational
121	function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along `Y` is the integer `ord_Y(f) := v_Y(f)`. -/
122	
123	namespace Scheme.RationalMap
124	
125	/-- **Order of a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along a prime divisor `Y`.**
126	
127	For `X` satisfying Hartshorne's `(*)`, the local ring `O_{X,η}` at the generic
128	point `η` of `Y` is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field the function
129	field `K(X)`, and `ord_Y(f) = v_Y(f) ∈ ℤ` is the value of the associated normalised
130	discrete valuation on `f`.
131	
132	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, every closed point `P ∈ C` is a prime
133	divisor and `ord_P(f) = v_P(f)` is the standard DVR valuation at `P`.
134	
135	Blueprint reference: `def:order_at_point` (Hartshorne II §6 pp. 130–131; Stacks 02ME).

138	`addVal` (`IsDiscreteValuationRing.addVal : R → ℕ∞`), extends it to the fraction field
139	`K(X)`, and produces an `ℤ`-valued valuation on `K(X)^×`. -/
140	noncomputable def order {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
141	    (f : X.functionField) : ℤ :=
142	  sorry

150	/-! ## §3. Divisor of a closed point on a curve
151	
152	On a smooth proper curve `C` over a field, every closed point `P ∈ C` is a prime
153	divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the one-dimensional integral
154	scheme `C`). The associated Weil divisor is `[P] = 1 · P ∈ Div(C)`. -/
155	
156	/-- **The Weil divisor associated to a closed point `P` on a curve.** The element
157	`[P] := 1 · P ∈ Div(C)`, i.e. the prime divisor `P` with multiplicity one.
158	
159	For a smooth proper curve every closed point is automatically a prime divisor (it
160	is a codimension-one integral closed subscheme of the one-dimensional integral
161	scheme `C`); conversely every prime divisor on a curve is of this form, so an
162	arbitrary divisor on `C` is a finite formal `ℤ`-linear combination
163	`Σ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]` of closed points.
164	
165	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_closed_point` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 137).
166	
167	iter-174 body: the function is junk-defined outside its intended scope. We
168	case-split on `Order.coheight P = 1` (the codimension-one witness of
169	`PrimeDivisor`). On the branch where the equality holds — automatic for a
170	closed point on a one-dimensional integral scheme — we promote `P` to a
171	`PrimeDivisor` via the witness and return `Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1`, i.e.
172	the prime divisor `P` with multiplicity one. On the off-branch (junk regime)
173	we return the zero divisor. The blueprint pins the well-definedness argument
174	"`IsClosed {P}` on a one-dimensional integral scheme ⟹ `coheight P = 1`" as a
175	separate threadable hypothesis at the call site (chapter L330–L340 "Lean
176	signature scope"); see `ofClosedPoint_eq_single` for the equation in the
177	hypothesised regime. -/
178	noncomputable def ofClosedPoint {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
179	    (_hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) : C.WeilDivisor :=
180	  if h : Order.coheight P = 1 then Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1 else 0
181	
182	/-- In the hypothesised regime where the closed point `P` has coheight one
183	(the codim-1 condition automatic for a closed point of a one-dimensional
184	integral scheme), `ofClosedPoint P hP` is the prime divisor `P` with
185	multiplicity one. -/
186	lemma ofClosedPoint_eq_single {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
187	    (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) (h : Order.coheight P = 1) :
188	    ofClosedPoint P hP = Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1 := by
189	  simp [ofClosedPoint, h]
190	
191	/-- Off-branch: outside the codim-1 regime, `ofClosedPoint` is junk-defined as
192	the zero divisor. (Only relevant when the user supplies a "closed point" that
193	does not have coheight one in the ambient scheme — e.g. a generic point or a
194	codim-≥2 point on a higher-dimensional scheme.) -/
195	lemma ofClosedPoint_eq_zero {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
196	    (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) (h : Order.coheight P ≠ 1) :
197	    ofClosedPoint P hP = 0 := by
198	  simp [ofClosedPoint, h]
199	
200	/-! ## §4. Degree of a divisor over an algebraically closed base -/
201	
202	/-- **Degree of a Weil divisor on a smooth proper curve over `k̄`.**
203	
204	Over an algebraically closed field `k̄`, every closed point of a smooth proper
205	curve `C` has residue field `k̄`, so each prime divisor `[P]` contributes degree
206	one to the sum, and `deg(D) := Σᵢ nᵢ` is the sum of the integer coefficients of
207	the formal sum `D = Σ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]`.
208	
209	(Over a general field `k` one weights by the residue-field degrees
210	`Σᵢ nᵢ · [κ(Pᵢ) : k]` to recover the geometric degree; the project's RR bridge
211	needs only the `k̄` specialisation.)
212	
213	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_degree` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 137; Stacks 0BE0).
214	
215	Implementation: `Finsupp.sum D (fun _ n => n)` is the sum of all coefficients of
216	the finitely supported function representing `D`. -/
217	noncomputable def degree (D : X.WeilDivisor) : ℤ :=
218	  (D : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ).sum (fun _ n => n)
219	
220	/-- **The degree map is a group homomorphism `Div(C) → ℤ`.**
221	
222	`deg(D₁ + D₂) = deg(D₁) + deg(D₂)`, `deg(-D) = -deg(D)`, `deg(0) = 0`. Bundled
223	as an `AddMonoidHom` for downstream use (the linear-equivalence quotient
224	`Cl(C) := Div(C) / im(div)` will inherit a `deg : Cl(C) → ℤ` from this).
225	
226	Blueprint reference: `thm:divisor_degree_hom` (immediate from `def:divisor_degree`).
227	
228	Implementation: built from `Finsupp.liftAddHom (fun _ ↦ AddMonoidHom.id ℤ)`, the
229	generic Mathlib packaging that lifts a family of `AddMonoidHom`s indexed by the
230	support into a single `AddMonoidHom` on the finsupp. Unfolds to
231	`D.sum (fun _ z ↦ z) = degree D` (see `degree_hom_apply` below). -/
232	noncomputable def degree_hom : X.WeilDivisor →+ ℤ :=
233	  Finsupp.liftAddHom (fun _ ↦ AddMonoidHom.id ℤ)
234	
235	@[simp]
236	lemma degree_hom_apply (D : X.WeilDivisor) : degree_hom D = degree D :=
237	  Finsupp.liftAddHom_apply (α := X.PrimeDivisor) (M := ℤ) (N := ℤ)
238	    (fun _ ↦ AddMonoidHom.id ℤ) D
239	
240	/-! ## §5. Principal divisors -/
241	
242	/-- **The principal divisor of a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×`.**
243	
244	By Hartshorne's Lemma 6.1, `ord_Y(f) = 0` for all but finitely many prime
245	divisors `Y`, so the formal sum
246	`div(f) := Σ_{Y prime divisor} ord_Y(f) · Y ∈ Div(X)`
247	has finite support and is a well-defined Weil divisor.
248	
249	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, this specialises to
250	`div(f) = Σ_{P closed point} ord_P(f) · [P]`.
251	
252	Blueprint reference: `def:principal_divisor` (Hartshorne II §6 Lemma 6.1 +
253	following definition, p. 131).
254	
255	iter-173+: the body invokes Hartshorne 6.1 for the finite-support side condition,
256	then materialises the `Finsupp` whose value at each prime divisor `Y` is
257	`Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f`. -/
258	noncomputable def principal [IsIntegral X] (f : X.functionField)
259	    (_hf : f ≠ 0) : X.WeilDivisor :=
260	  sorry
261	
262	/-- **The principal-divisor map is a group homomorphism `K(X)^× → Div(X)`.**
263	
264	Concretely `div(fg) = div(f) + div(g)`, `div(f⁻¹) = -div(f)`, `div(1) = 0`. Bundled
265	as a `MonoidHom` from the multiplicative units of `K(X)` to `Multiplicative (Div(X))`
266	(equivalently: an additive map `(K(X)^×, ·) → (Div(X), +)`).
267	
268	Blueprint reference: `thm:principal_hom` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 131).
269	
270	iter-173+: closes coordinate-wise from the DVR identities `v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)`,
271	`v_Y(f⁻¹) = -v_Y(f)`, `v_Y(1) = 0` (`AddValuation.map_add`, etc.), summing over the
272	union of supports. -/
273	noncomputable def principal_hom [IsIntegral X] :
274	    (X.functionField)ˣ →* Multiplicative X.WeilDivisor :=
275	  sorry
276	
277	/-- **Principal divisors on a complete nonsingular curve have degree zero**
278	(Hartshorne Corollary II.6.10, Stacks 0BE3).
279	
280	For every nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(C)^×` on a smooth proper curve `C`
281	over an algebraically closed field `k̄`,
282	`deg(div(f)) = 0 ∈ ℤ`.
283	
284	Blueprint reference: `thm:principal_deg_zero` (Hartshorne II.6 Cor. 6.10 p. 138).
285	
286	The proof (Hartshorne 6.10): if `f ∈ k̄` is constant then `div(f) = 0` and the
287	claim is trivial. Otherwise the inclusion `k̄(f) ⊂ K(C)` exhibits `K(C)` as a
288	finite extension of `k̄(f) ≅ k̄(t)`, so the corresponding morphism
289	`φ : C → ℙ¹_{k̄}` is finite, `div(f) = φ^*([0] - [∞])`, and pullback along a
290	finite morphism multiplies degree by `deg(φ)`. Two auxiliary sub-lemmas
291	(finite morphism induced by a non-constant rational function; multiplicativity
292	of degree under finite pullback, Hartshorne II.6.9) are deferred to follow-up
293	iters of `RR.1`. -/
294	theorem principal_degree_zero {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
295	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
296	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
297	    [IsIntegral C.left] (f : C.left.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0) :
298	    degree (principal f hf) = 0 :=
299	  sorry
300	
301	/-! ## §6. Linear equivalence and the divisor class group -/
302	
303	/-- **Linear equivalence of Weil divisors.**
304	
305	Two Weil divisors `D, D' ∈ Div(X)` are linearly equivalent, written `D ~ D'`, if
306	and only if there exists a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×` with
307	`D - D' = div(f) ∈ Div(X)`.
308	
309	`~` is an equivalence relation (reflexivity from `div(1) = 0`, symmetry from
310	`div(f⁻¹) = -div(f)`, transitivity from `div(fg) = div(f) + div(g)`, all via
311	`thm:principal_hom`). The quotient `Cl(X) := Div(X) / im(div)` is the
312	**divisor class group** of `X`.
313	
314	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, `thm:principal_deg_zero` shows the
315	degree map descends to `deg : Cl(C) → ℤ`.
316	
317	Blueprint reference: `def:linear_equivalence` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 131). -/
318	def LinearEquivalence [IsIntegral X] (D D' : X.WeilDivisor) : Prop :=
319	  ∃ (f : X.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0), D - D' = principal f hf
320	
321	end Scheme.WeilDivisor
322	
323	end AlgebraicGeometry

1	# Analogy: Mathlib API path for `Scheme.RationalMap.order`
2	
3	## Mode
4	api-alignment
5	
6	## Slug
7	dvr-rationalmap-order
8	
9	## Iteration
10	175
11	
12	## Question
13	For the body of `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order`
14	(`AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean` L140), what is the correct
15	Mathlib API path to (a) extract the discrete valuation at a codim-1 point of
16	a (locally noetherian, regular-in-codim-1) integral scheme, (b) extend that
17	valuation from the local ring to the fraction field, and (c) handle the
18	junk-on-`f = 0` convention?
19	
20	## Project artifact(s)
21	- `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean`:140-142 — `RationalMap.order` body sorry.
22	- `blueprint/src/chapters/RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex`, `def:order_at_point` block (statement-only `\leanok`).
23	- `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean`:80-97 — `Scheme.PrimeDivisor`
24	  carrier (`coheight : Order.coheight point = 1`).
25	
26	## Decisions identified
27	
28	### Decision 1: Use `Ring.ordFrac` vs. roll-your-own DVR-`addVal`-then-extend
29	
30	- **Mathlib idiom**: `Ring.ordFrac R : K →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰` (with `ℤᵐ⁰ = WithZero (Multiplicative ℤ)`),
31	  defined at `Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Basic:324` (Stacks `02MD`). The
32	  hypotheses are exactly `[CommRing R] [Nontrivial R] [IsNoetherianRing R]
33	  [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 R]` plus `[Field K] [Algebra R K] [IsFractionRing R K]`.
34	  Internally `ordFrac` extends `Ring.ordMonoidWithZeroHom R : R →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰` to the
35	  fraction field via `Submonoid.LocalizationMap.lift₀` (the `lift₀` already
36	  handles the units-mapping discharge). The integer value on `r ∈ R` is the
37	  length of `R / (r)` (= the DVR valuation for an `r`-uniformised local DVR);
38	  on `a/b ∈ K`, it is `ord(a) - ord(b)` (in `Multiplicative ℤ` terms).
39	  Why Mathlib chose it: the natural codim-1 generality covers BOTH the
40	  global-Noetherian / Krull-dim-1 case (Dedekind domains, regular curves) and
41	  the strictly local DVR case; one definition serves both — the DVR
42	  `addVal` is then derivable as a corollary, not a prerequisite. The
43	  intermediate length-of-quotient definition `Ring.ord` (Stacks 02MD) is what
44	  lets the same formula work in any Krull-dim-≤-1 ring (not just DVRs).
45	- **Project's current path**: bare `sorry` body (iter-172 file-skeleton); the
46	  iter-174 docstring comment proposes "extract DVR addVal then extend to
47	  fraction field", which is the parallel-API path.
48	- **Gap**: divergent-with-cost (project's proposed path is parallel API; the
49	  shipped body has nothing yet, so the cost is *avoidance* of the parallel
50	  build, not refactor of shipped code).
51	- **Cost of divergence (if Project goes its own way)**:
52	  - 4–6 bridge lemmas to materialise an `AddValuation` on the stalk and lift
53	    to the fraction field (`IsDiscreteValuationRing.addVal` produces
54	    `AddValuation R ℕ∞`; `AddValuation.ofValuation` round-trips through
55	    `Valuation R (Multiplicative ℕ∞ᵒᵈ)`; `Valuation.extendToLocalization`
56	    needs the supp-disjoint side condition; finally the `ℕ∞ → ℤ` cast adds
57	    a junk choice on `⊤`). Each step is a separate definition and proof.
58	  - Downstream: `principal` (`Mathlib`-style `K → Div(X)` Finsupp) cannot
59	    consume `IsDedekindDomain.HeightOneSpectrum.valuation`-style adic
60	    valuations directly; it gets a project-bespoke `addVal` that must be
61	    bridged again at every Hartshorne-style identity (`v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) +
62	    v_Y(g)`, etc.).
63	  - Loses access to `Ring.ord_mul`, `ord_pow`, `ord_of_isUnit`,
64	    `ordMonoidWithZeroHom_eq_ord`, `ordFrac_eq_div` — every algebraic
65	    identity needed for `thm:principal_hom` would be re-proved.
66	- **Verdict**: **ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB** — the body must use `Ring.ordFrac`.
67	
68	### Decision 2: Junk-on-`f = 0` convention (`ℤᵐ⁰ → ℤ` extraction)
69	
70	- **Mathlib idiom**: `WithZero.log : ℤᵐ⁰ → ℤ` from
71	  `Mathlib.Algebra.GroupWithZero.WithZero:394`, defined as
72	  `x.recZeroCoe 0 Multiplicative.toAdd`. Properties:
73	  - `log_zero : log 0 = 0` (the junk-on-zero convention)
74	  - `log_exp : log (exp a) = a` (round-trip on nonzero)
75	  - `log_mul : log (x * y) = log x + log y` for `x, y ≠ 0` (the additive
76	    valuation identity that `thm:principal_hom` will downstream consume).
77	  This is the *named* Mathlib idiom for converting `ℤᵐ⁰` (the multiplicative
78	  value group with junk-zero) to the additive `ℤ` value group with junk-zero.
79	  The dual `WithTop ℤ → ℤ` is `WithTop.untop₀` (used by
80	  `MeromorphicOn.divisor` at `Mathlib.Analysis.Meromorphic.Divisor`); for our
81	  multiplicative-value-group case `WithZero.log` is the direct match.
82	  Why Mathlib chose it: it makes the junk choice canonical and reusable, so
83	  every downstream identity (e.g. `log_mul`, `log_pow`) lands as a `simp`
84	  lemma rather than a per-call-site case-split.
85	- **Project's current path**: not chosen yet (statement-only).
86	- **Gap**: identical (no prior project pin to compare).
87	- **Verdict**: **ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB** — use `WithZero.log`.
88	
89	### Decision 3: DVR-from-codim-1 chain (closing the typeclass synthesis)
90	
91	- **Mathlib idiom**:
92	  - `AlgebraicGeometry.instIsNoetherianRingCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsLocallyNoetherian`
93	    (`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Noetherian`): for `[IsLocallyNoetherian X]`,
94	    the stalk is `IsNoetherianRing`.
95	  - `AlgebraicGeometry.instIsDomainCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsIntegral`
96	    (`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.FunctionField`): for `[IsIntegral X]`, every
97	    stalk is `IsDomain` (hence `Nontrivial`).
98	  - `AlgebraicGeometry.instIsFractionRingCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafFunctionField`
99	    (`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.FunctionField`): for `[IsIntegral X]`, the
100	    function field is a fraction ring of the stalk at every point — so
101	    `[IsFractionRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) X.functionField]` is
102	    automatic.
103	  - For Krull-dim-1: `IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim_eq_height`
104	    (`Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height`) + `IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_stalk`
105	    (`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme`) gives
106	    `ringKrullDim (stalk) = primeIdealOf(x).height`.
107	- **Project's current path**: the chapter pre-pins (iter-173 prose, blueprint
108	  L98–L112) the project-side conjunction
109	  `∀ x, Order.coheight x = 1 → IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk x)`,
110	  not yet bundled as a typeclass.
111	- **Gap**: divergent-and-incomplete — **Mathlib has no direct
112	  `Order.coheight x = 1 → Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk x)` bridge**.
113	  The missing fact is the topological identity "coheight of `x` in the
114	  scheme's specialisation preorder equals the height of the prime ideal
115	  corresponding to `x` in any affine chart" (Stacks `02IZ` / `005X`). The
116	  step "ideal height → stalk Krull dim" is in Mathlib
117	  (`IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim_eq_height`); the topological-to-
118	  prime-spectrum bridge is the gap.
119	- **Cost of divergence**: low for iter-175 — the workaround is to
120	  thread `[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]` (or the explicit
121	  `[IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]` if downstream cares)
122	  as an explicit instance/hypothesis on the `order` signature at iter-175;
123	  the topological bridge can be deferred to a later iter or upstreamed to
124	  Mathlib as a small PR.
125	- **Verdict**: **NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL** for the topological coheight bridge;
126	  **PROCEED** with explicit-instance threading for iter-175.
127	
128	## Recommendation
129	
130	**Body of `RationalMap.order` (iter-175 prover lane):**
131	
132	```lean
133	noncomputable def order {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X]
134	    [IsLocallyNoetherian X] (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
135	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]
136	    (f : X.functionField) : ℤ :=
137	  WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) f)
138	```
139	
140	Rationale per decision:
141	
142	1. **`Ring.ordFrac`** is the canonical Mathlib API for "extension of the DVR
143	   `addVal` to the fraction field, in a way that respects the units-on-`R^×`
144	   and gives a `K →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰` monoid-with-zero hom". It works for ANY Noetherian
145	   Krull-dim-≤-1 commutative ring; under the project's additional
146	   `regular-in-codim-1` standing hypothesis the same value coincides with
147	   `IsDiscreteValuationRing.addVal` cast to `ℤ` (via the chain
148	   `ordFrac_eq_ord` ⟹ `ordMonoidWithZeroHom_eq_ord` ⟹ DVR `addVal_uniformizer`).
149	2. **`WithZero.log`** is the canonical `ℤᵐ⁰ → ℤ` projection with junk-on-zero;
150	   `log 0 = 0`, and `log_mul`/`log_pow` give the additive-valuation algebra
151	   identities `thm:principal_hom` will downstream consume for free.
152	3. **Typeclass threading**: add `[IsLocallyNoetherian X]` and
153	   `[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]` as instance arguments on
154	   the `order` signature. The `IsFractionRing` instance is automatic from
155	   `[IsIntegral X]`; `Nontrivial` of the stalk is automatic from
156	   `IsDomain`. The codim-1 stalk instance is the project-side workaround for
157	   the missing Mathlib topological bridge (Decision 3).
158	
159	**Downstream impact (for `principal`, `principal_hom`):**
160	
161	- `principal f hf` becomes `Finsupp.onFinset`-style construction with
162	  Hartshorne 6.1 supplying finite support; values are
163	  `order Y f = WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac _ f)`.
164	- `principal_hom` reduces to `log_mul` / `log_one` on the underlying `ℤᵐ⁰`
165	  values, applied prime-divisor-wise.
166	- `principal_degree_zero`'s sub-build (Hartshorne 6.10) is unchanged by this
167	  decision — the proof goes through the finite morphism into `ℙ¹` either way.
168	
169	**Blueprint-side gap closure (writer's task this plan-phase):**
170	
171	The `def:order_at_point` block should add a "Lean encoding" paragraph pinning
172	the API choice as:
173	
174	> Lean encoding: `order Y f := WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk
175	> Y.point) f)`, where `Ring.ordFrac` is Mathlib's monoid-with-zero hom
176	> `K →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰` from `Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Basic` (Stacks 02MD).
177	> The junk-on-`f = 0` convention is `order Y 0 = 0`, inherited from
178	> `WithZero.log 0 = 0`. The Krull-dim-1 hypothesis on the stalk is threaded
179	> as `[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]` (the topological
180	> coheight → stalk Krull-dim bridge is Mathlib-upstream-pending; see
181	> Stacks 02IZ).
182	
183	**Mathlib upstream opportunity (optional, not iter-175 blocking):**
184	
185	A two-line PR to Mathlib pinning the bridge
186	`Order.coheight (x : (Spec R)) = (asIdeal x).height` (and the `Scheme`-level
187	corollary via `IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_stalk`) would let the project
188	**drop** the explicit `[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 _]` argument and synthesise it
189	purely from `Order.coheight Y.point = 1` (via the structure carrier). This
190	is the cleanest long-term shape but is *not* on the iter-175 critical path.

3	\chapter{Weil divisors on a smooth proper curve (RR.1)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II \S6, pp.~130--137 +
7	% IV.1, pp.~294--296 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
8	% Stacks pointers (tag-level, not verbatim-quoted): 02RW (divisors),
9	% 02ME (order at a point on a regular 1-dimensional scheme),
10	% 0BE0 (degree of a divisor on a curve), 0BE3 (principal divisors have
11	% degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve).
12	
13	\section{Setup and motivation}
14	
15	This chapter is the first of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
16	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
17	\[
18	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
19	  \,\colon\,
20	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ with
21	  $g(C) = 0$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.}
22	\]
23	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
24	divisor of a closed point on a curve (\texttt{RR.1}, this chapter); the
25	Riemann--Roch dimension formula $\ell(D) = \deg D + 1$ for $\deg D \geq 0$ on
26	a genus-$0$ curve (\texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections argument identifying
27	$\dim_k H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ via $1$ and a non-constant function
28	(\texttt{RR.3}); the ``rational curve $\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1$''
29	classification recovered as a degree-$1$ morphism into $\mathbb P^1$ via
30	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
31	
32	Mathlib ships no \texttt{WeilDivisor} on a scheme. Adjacent pieces exist, but
33	none of them is the data structure we need: \texttt{MeromorphicOn.divisor}
34	(\texttt{Mathlib.Analysis.Meromorphic.Divisor}) is the order function of a
35	meromorphic function valued in a \emph{normed} field, not a formal sum of
36	codimension-$1$ cycles on a scheme; \texttt{CommRing.Pic}
37	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) is the ring-level Picard group of
38	invertible modules under tensor product, with no scheme-level analogue;
39	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap}
40	(\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}) packages rational
41	maps but not divisors. Consequently this chapter, and the three siblings, are
42	\emph{project-bespoke}: the only Mathlib tools they rely on are
43	discrete-valuation rings, the function field, and the ambient scheme API.
44	The audit grounding this scope decision is in
45	\texttt{analogies/rrbridge-survey.md}.
46	
47	This chapter establishes the formal-sum data type
48	$\Div(X) = \bigoplus_{\substack{Z \subset X \\ \codim 1}} \mathbb Z$ on a
49	Noetherian integral scheme $X$ satisfying Hartshorne's condition $(*)$, the
50	principal-divisor homomorphism $\div \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \Div(X)$ on a
51	curve, the degree map $\deg \colon \Div(C) \to \mathbb Z$ on a smooth proper
52	curve over an algebraically closed field, and the linear-equivalence
53	relation. The \emph{degree-zero of a principal divisor} statement
54	(\Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero}) is included for completeness; its proof
55	decomposes into a separate sub-build inside \texttt{RR.1} (it uses the
56	auxiliary lemma that any non-constant rational function on $C$ defines a
57	finite morphism $C \to \mathbb P^1$). The line-bundle construction $\mathcal
58	O_C(D)$ and the associated cohomology computation $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))$
59	live in the sibling chapter \texttt{RR.3}; the Riemann--Roch dimension
60	formula lives in \texttt{RR.2}; the genus-$0$ classification proper lives in
61	\texttt{RR.4}.
62	
63	Throughout this chapter, the standing convention is Hartshorne's condition
64	$(*)$:
65	
66	% SOURCE QUOTE: "$(\ast)$ $X$ is a noetherian integral separated scheme which
67	% is regular in codimension one."
68	\textit{Source: Hartshorne, II \S6, p.~130, the conditional $(*)$.}
69	\[
70	  (\ast)\qquad
71	  X \text{ is a noetherian integral separated scheme which is regular in
72	  codimension one.}
73	\]
74	For the degree map and for principal-divisor statements on a curve, we
75	additionally assume $X$ is a complete (i.e.\ proper) nonsingular curve over
76	an algebraically closed field $\bar k$, where Hartshorne's condition $(*)$ is
77	automatic.
78	
79	\paragraph{Standing hypothesis $(*)$ in the Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).}
80	Mathlib (as of \texttt{b80f227}) does not bundle Hartshorne's four-clause
81	$(\ast)$ as a single typeclass. The chapter pins $(*)$ to a \emph{layered}
82	typeclass set that the prover-agent threads through the Lean signatures in
83	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean}:
84	
85	\begin{itemize}
86	  \item \textbf{Basic formal-sum layer} (carrier of \Cref{def:prime_divisor} and
87	    \Cref{def:codim1_cycles}, the additive group $\Div(X)$, the degree
88	    \Cref{def:divisor_degree}, the degree-homomorphism
89	    \Cref{thm:divisor_degree_hom}, and \Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}): the
90	    only Mathlib typeclass invoked is \texttt{[IsIntegral X]}
91	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme} via
92	    \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Properties}). Integrality of $X$ already
93	    delivers \texttt{IrreducibleSpace X.carrier} (the carrier-level instance
94	    chain), so $X$ has a unique generic point and the function field
95	    \texttt{X.functionField} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.FunctionField})
96	    is a field; separatedness and noetherianness are \emph{not} required for
97	    the bare formal-sum data type.
98	  \item \textbf{Order/principal layer} (\Cref{def:order_at_point},
99	    \Cref{def:principal_divisor}, \Cref{thm:principal_hom}): the Lean
100	    signature additionally threads
101	    \texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian X]}
102	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Noetherian}) and Hartshorne's
103	    regular-in-codimension-one clause. The latter has \emph{no} Mathlib
104	    typeclass shipping it directly; the chapter's pin is the project-side
105	    predicate ``for every $x \in X$ with $\codim_X \overline{\{x\}} = 1$ the
106	    stalk $\mathcal O_{X,x}$ is a discrete valuation ring'', encodable as
107	    the conjunction
108	    $\forall x,\; \texttt{Order.coheight}\,x = 1 \Rightarrow

111	    abbreviate this; until then the order/principal lemmas thread the DVR
112	    hypothesis explicitly at each codim-1 point.
113	  \item \textbf{Curve layer} (\Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero}): the standing
114	    hypothesis tightens to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
115	    over $\bar k$ with explicit integrality witness, namely
116	    \begin{quote}
117	      \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
118	       (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
119	       \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\

145	  \label{def:prime_divisor}
146	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor}
147	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (definition of "prime divisor" under
148	  % condition $(*)$) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
149	  % PDF page 147). Stacks pointer: tag 02RW (divisors).
150	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let $X$ satisfy $(\ast)$. A \emph{prime
151	  % divisor} on $X$ is a closed integral subscheme $Y$ of codimension one."
152	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (definition of prime divisor).}
153	  Let $X$ satisfy $(*)$. A \emph{prime divisor} on $X$ is a closed integral
154	  subscheme $Y \subset X$ of codimension one. Equivalently, by the standard
155	  generic-point correspondence (Hartshorne II \S6, p.~130, ``let $\eta \in
156	  Y$ be its generic point\ldots''), $Y$ is determined by its generic point
157	  $\eta \in X$: closed integral subschemes of codimension one of $X$ are in
158	  natural bijection with points $x \in X$ such that the closure
159	  $\overline{\{x\}} \subset X$ has codimension one. The closure
160	  $\overline{\{x\}}$, equipped with the \emph{reduced induced subscheme
161	  structure}, is automatically integral (irreducible, because the closure
162	  of a single point is irreducible, and reduced by construction); the only
163	  non-automatic condition is the codimension.
164	
165	  \paragraph{Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).} The Lean structure
166	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor} bundles a point
167	  \texttt{point : X} together with a codimension-one witness, encoded via
168	  the Mathlib predicate
169	  \[
170	    \texttt{Order.coheight\ point\ =\ 1}
171	  \]
172	  on the specialisation preorder of the topological space underlying $X$.
173	  Concretely, Mathlib's preorder on \texttt{X.carrier} is defined by
174	  ``$x \leq y \iff y \rightsquigarrow x$'' (i.e.\ $x \in \overline{\{y\}}$;
175	  see \texttt{Mathlib.Topology.Specialization} and
176	  \texttt{Mathlib.Order.KrullDimension}). Under this convention the generic
177	  point of an integral scheme is the unique \emph{maximal} element of the
178	  preorder, so for the generic point $\eta_Y$ of a prime divisor $Y$ the
179	  only point strictly above $\eta_Y$ is the generic point $\eta_X$ of $X$,
180	  and the chain $\eta_Y < \eta_X$ has length one, giving
181	  \texttt{Order.coheight\ $\eta_Y$\ =\ 1}. The DVR/integrality content of
182	  ``the closure of \texttt{point} with the reduced subscheme structure is
183	  integral'' is automatic from $\overline{\{\,\texttt{point}\,\}}$ being
184	  irreducible, so the Lean structure does not need a separate integrality
185	  field. The iter-172 placeholder \texttt{isCodim1AndIntegral : True} in
186	  \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean:84--90} is to be
187	  refined to \texttt{coheight : Order.coheight point = 1} (named field, type
188	  \texttt{Prop}) in the iter-173 prover step, retaining the same
189	  \texttt{point} field for downstream defeq.
190	
191	  \paragraph{Reference predicates considered and rejected.}
192	  Two alternatives were considered and rejected at the iter-172
193	  lean-vs-blueprint-checker stage:
194	  \begin{itemize}
195	    \item \texttt{IsIntegral\ (X.closedSubschemeOfPoint\ x)}: rejected
196	      because Mathlib (\texttt{b80f227}) ships \emph{no}
197	      \texttt{closedSubschemeOfPoint} constructor. The closest available
198	      object is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData}
199	      (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf.Basic}), which packages
200	      a quasi-coherent ideal sheaf but does not produce a reduced induced
201	      subscheme on the closure of a point. Building that constructor is
202	      itself a Mathlib-upstream-scale project (Stacks tag 01J9 ``reduced
203	      induced scheme structure''), outside the scope of \texttt{RR.1}.
204	    \item A bundled \texttt{Scheme.IdealSheafData}-based prime-divisor
205	      carrier: rejected because the chapter only needs the generic-point
206	      witness for downstream order/principal-divisor consumption; carrying
207	      an \texttt{IdealSheafData} adds redundant data the chapter never
208	      uses.
209	  \end{itemize}
210	  The \texttt{Order.coheight}-based encoding is therefore the
211	  minimum-Mathlib-dependence pin compatible with the chapter's downstream
212	  needs.
213	\end{definition}

226	  % group $\operatorname{Div} X$ generated by the prime divisors. We write a
227	  % divisor as $D = \sum n_i Y_i$, where the $Y_i$ are prime divisors, the
228	  % $n_i$ are integers, and only finitely many $n_i$ are different from

256	\begin{definition}
257	
258	\leanok
259	[Order of a rational function at a prime divisor]
260	  \label{def:order_at_point}
261	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order}
262	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
263	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130--131 (valuation $v_Y$ of a prime
264	  % divisor; "zero/pole of $f$ along $Y$") (read from
265	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 147--148).
266	  % Stacks pointer: tag 02ME.
267	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If $Y$ is a prime divisor on $X$, let $\eta \in Y$ be its
268	  % generic point. Then the local ring $\mathcal O_{\eta, X}$ of $X$ at
269	  % $\eta$ is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field $K$, the
270	  % function field of $X$. We call the corresponding discrete valuation
271	  % $v_Y$ the \emph{valuation of $Y$}."
272	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now let $f \in K^\ast$ be any nonzero rational function
273	  % on $X$. Then $v_Y(f)$ is an integer. If it is positive, we say $f$ has
274	  % a \emph{zero} along $Y$, of that order; if it is negative, we say $f$
275	  % has a \emph{pole} along $Y$, of order $-v_Y(f)$."
276	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, pp.~130--131 (valuation $v_Y$).}
277	  Let $X$ satisfy $(*)$. For a prime divisor $Y \subset X$ with generic
278	  point $\eta \in Y$, the local ring $\mathcal O_{X, \eta}$ is a discrete
279	  valuation ring (by regularity in codimension one) with quotient field the
280	  function field $K(X)$. For a nonzero rational function $f \in K(X)^{\times}$
281	  the \emph{order of $f$ along $Y$} is the integer
282	  \[
283	    \ord_Y(f) \;:=\; v_Y(f) \;\in\; \mathbb Z,
284	  \]
285	  where $v_Y$ is the normalised discrete valuation associated to
286	  $\mathcal O_{X, \eta}$. If $\ord_Y(f) > 0$, $f$ has a \emph{zero of
287	  order $\ord_Y(f)$ along $Y$}; if $\ord_Y(f) < 0$, $f$ has a \emph{pole
288	  of order $-\ord_Y(f)$ along $Y$}.
289	
290	  Specialisation to a curve. If $C$ is a smooth proper curve over $\bar k$
291	  and $P \in C$ is a closed point, then $P$ is automatically a prime
292	  divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the $1$-dimensional
293	  scheme $C$); the local ring $\mathcal O_{C, P}$ is a discrete valuation
294	  ring by smoothness, and $\ord_P(f) = v_P(f)$ is the standard DVR
295	  valuation of $f$ at $P$.
296	
297	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope (iter-175 pin, per analogist
298	  \texttt{dvr-rationalmap-order}).} The iter-175 Lean signature is
299	  \begin{quote}
300	    \texttt{noncomputable def order \{X : Scheme.\{u\}\} [IsIntegral X]\\
301	    \phantom{}\quad[IsLocallyNoetherian X] (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)\\
302	    \phantom{}\quad[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]\\
303	    \phantom{}\quad(f : X.functionField) : $\mathbb Z$}
304	  \end{quote}
305	  with \emph{no} $f \neq 0$ hypothesis (junk-on-$f = 0$ convention,
306	  see below). The body is
307	  \[
308	    \texttt{order}\ Y\ f \;:=\;
309	      \texttt{WithZero.log}\ \bigl(
310	        \texttt{Ring.ordFrac}\ (X.\texttt{presheaf.stalk}\ Y.\texttt{point})\ f
311	      \bigr).
312	  \]
313	  \emph{Mathlib API path.}
314	  \begin{itemize}
315	    \item \texttt{Ring.ordFrac R : K $\to\!\!*_0$ $\mathbb Z^{m_0}$}
316	      (with $\mathbb Z^{m_0} = \texttt{WithZero (Multiplicative}\ \mathbb Z)$),
317	      from \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Basic} (Stacks
318	      tag~\texttt{02MD}), is Mathlib's canonical monoid-with-zero hom
319	      extending the length-of-quotient valuation
320	      \texttt{Ring.ord} from a Noetherian Krull-dim-$\leq 1$ ring to
321	      its fraction field via \texttt{Submonoid.LocalizationMap.lift\textsubscript{0}}.
322	      For a DVR-uniformised local ring its value on $r \in R$ is the
323	      length of $R/(r)$ (the DVR valuation); on $a/b \in K$, it is
324	      $\texttt{ord}(a) - \texttt{ord}(b)$. The hypotheses
325	      \texttt{[CommRing R] [Nontrivial R] [IsNoetherianRing R]\\
326	      [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 R]} are exactly the typeclasses the body
327	      synthesises from $[\texttt{IsIntegral}\ X]$ (delivers
328	      \texttt{IsDomain} hence \texttt{Nontrivial} on the stalk via
329	      \texttt{instIsDomainCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsIntegral},
330	      and \texttt{IsFractionRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)
331	      X.functionField} via
332	      \texttt{instIsFractionRingCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafFunctionField}),
333	      from $[\texttt{IsLocallyNoetherian}\ X]$ (delivers
334	      \texttt{IsNoetherianRing} on the stalk via
335	      \texttt{instIsNoetherianRingCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsLocallyNoetherian}),
336	      and from the explicitly threaded
337	      \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]}.
338	    \item \texttt{WithZero.log : $\mathbb Z^{m_0} \to \mathbb Z$}
339	      from \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.GroupWithZero.WithZero} is Mathlib's
340	      canonical projection to the additive value group with
341	      junk-on-zero: \texttt{log\_zero}: $\log 0 = 0$;
342	      \texttt{log\_mul}: $\log(x \cdot y) = \log x + \log y$ for
343	      $x, y \neq 0$. This makes the downstream additive identities
344	      $v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)$, $v_Y(f^{-1}) = -v_Y(f)$,
345	      $v_Y(1) = 0$ available as named lemmas for
346	      \Cref{thm:principal_hom}.
347	  \end{itemize}
348	
349	  \emph{Junk-on-$f = 0$ convention.} Setting $f = 0$ in
350	  \texttt{X.functionField} feeds $0$ into
351	  \texttt{Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)} (zero of the
352	  monoid-with-zero hom; \texttt{Ring.ordFrac} maps $0$ to
353	  $0 \in \mathbb Z^{m_0}$); then \texttt{WithZero.log 0 = 0} so
354	  $\texttt{order}\ Y\ 0 = 0$. This is the canonical Mathlib junk
355	  choice; no separate $f \neq 0$ hypothesis is threaded on the
356	  signature.
357	
358	  \emph{In-tree gap-fill (project-side; Mathlib upstream opportunity).}
359	  Mathlib (as of \texttt{b80f227}) has no direct bridge from the
360	  topological condition $\texttt{Order.coheight}\ Y.\texttt{point} = 1$
361	  on \texttt{X.carrier} to the algebraic condition
362	  $\texttt{Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)}$ on the
363	  stalk. The missing fact (Stacks tag~\texttt{02IZ} / \texttt{005X}) is
364	  the topological identity ``coheight of $x$ in the scheme's
365	  specialisation preorder equals the height of the prime ideal
366	  corresponding to $x$ in any affine chart''; the algebraic step
367	  ``ideal height $=$ stalk Krull dim'' is in Mathlib as
368	  \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height}
369	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height}) and
370	  \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk}
371	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}). The iter-175
372	  workaround is to thread the explicit instance
373	  \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]} on
374	  \texttt{order} (and downstream consumers); a future two-line
375	  Mathlib upstream PR pinning the coheight-height bridge would let
376	  the project drop the explicit argument in favour of synthesis from
377	  \texttt{coheight Y.point = 1} via the \texttt{PrimeDivisor} carrier.

519	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
